data.title: Is It Time To Reassess HP (HPQ) After Sharp Short Term Share Price Gains?
data.description:
• Wondering whether HP's current share price reflects its true worth, or if the market is missing something important about this long established name in tech?
  • HP's share price recently closed at US$21.09, with returns of 11.1% over 7 days and 15.4% over 30 days, while the 1 year return sits at an 8.4% decline and the 5 year return at a 26.7% decline.
  • Recent coverage has focused on HP's position within the broader tech sector and how investors are reassessing established hardware and services businesses in light of changing spending patterns. This context helps explain why the stock has seen short term gains alongside weaker multi year returns, as the market weighs near term sentiment against a longer track record.
  • HP currently has a valuation score of 5/6. This indicates that five out of six checks suggest the shares may be priced below what the fundamentals imply. The sections ahead will break this down across different valuation methods, and will conclude with a way to look beyond the models to the story behind the numbers.

Approach 1: HP Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back into today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what all the future cash HP might generate is worth right now.

HP’s latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $2.97b. Analysts have provided forecasts out to 2030, with Simply Wall St extending those into a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model using cash flow projections through 2035. For example, projected Free Cash Flow in 2030 is $3.08b, and the model discounts each year’s figure back to today using a required rate of return.

When all those discounted cash flows are added together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $42.80 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $21.09, the DCF output implies an intrinsic discount of roughly 50.7%. This suggests HP may be trading at a substantial discount to this cash flow based estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests HP is undervalued by 50.7%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 58 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: HP Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like HP, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. A higher or lower P/E often reflects what the market is baking in for future growth and how much risk investors feel they are taking on.

Stronger growth and lower perceived risk usually support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tend to point to a lower “normal” or “fair” P/E range. That is why it helps to check HP’s current P/E against a few benchmarks rather than looking at the number in isolation.

HP currently trades on a P/E of 7.69x. The broader Tech industry average P/E is 23.70x, and the peer group average is 47.58x, so HP’s earnings multiple sits well below both of these reference points. Simply Wall St also calculates a “Fair Ratio” of 20.25x, which is the P/E that might be expected given HP’s earnings growth profile, margins, industry, market cap and risk factors.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple industry or peer comparison because it adjusts for company specific characteristics rather than assuming all tech names deserve the same multiple. Comparing the Fair Ratio of 20.25x with the actual P/E of 7.69x suggests the shares trade below this earnings based reference point.

Result: UNDERVALUED

data.title: Is Tripadvisor (TRIP) A Bargain After Recent 20% Share Price Jump?
data.description:

  • The stock is up 20.1% over the last month, while returns are negative over the past year at 5.6% and year to date at 23.3%.

Approach 1: Tripadvisor Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes Tripadvisor’s expected future cash flows and then discounts them back to today using a required rate of return to estimate what the business might be worth right now.

Tripadvisor’s latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $169.9 million. Using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, analyst estimates and extrapolations project Free Cash Flow reaching $322 million in 2030, with interim years such as 2026 and 2027 at $241.6 million and $258.4 million respectively. Simply Wall St only uses direct analyst estimates where available and then extends the series using its own growth assumptions to complete the 10 year path.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $33.48 per share. Against a current share price of roughly $11.23, this implies Tripadvisor appears around 66.5% undervalued on this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Tripadvisor is undervalued by 66.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 58 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Tripadvisor.

Approach 2: Tripadvisor Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It tends to reflect what the market is willing to pay today given expectations for future growth and the risk of those earnings.

Higher growth and lower perceived risk usually support a higher, or more generous, P/E ratio, while slower growth or higher uncertainty often go with a lower, or more cautious, multiple. Tripadvisor currently trades on a P/E of 32.22x. That is above both the Interactive Media and Services industry average of 16.04x and the peer group average of 20.47x.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Tripadvisor is 23.18x. This is a proprietary estimate of what Tripadvisor’s P/E “should” be, based on factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market value and risk characteristics. That makes it more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the sector, which may have very different growth, size or risk profiles.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 23.18x with the current P/E of 32.22x suggests the shares are trading above this modelled fair level.

  • This executive change affects the leadership overseeing financial reporting and operational finance across the business.

VNET Group, which operates data center and internet infrastructure services in China, sits in a sector where capital needs, utilization rates, and customer demand often put a spotlight on the finance function. When a CFO role changes hands, investors frequently pay close attention to continuity in reporting quality, internal controls, and access to funding. The new senior finance appointment places operational finance and accounting oversight at the center of that transition.

For you as an investor, the key questions are how smoothly responsibilities transfer and whether the new finance leadership maintains consistent financial disclosure and oversight. The CFO resignation and appointment of a new senior finance executive sit right at the heart of VNET Group's funding and reporting responsibilities. For a capital intensive data center operator, the finance team plays a central role in managing debt maturities, supporting growth projects, and maintaining investor confidence in reported numbers. The company has stated that the outgoing CFO's decision is for personal reasons and not linked to disagreements on operations or policies, which reduces the likelihood that this reflects a sudden break in financial direction. By naming a Senior Vice President, Operational Finance as principal accounting officer, VNET is signaling a focus on day to day financial discipline and compliance while broader capital structure decisions remain a key area for the wider leadership team.
